topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bouquet was offensively bright—a riot of crimson roses and yellow lilies, utterly superfluous. I stared at them on the counter, my jaw tightening until a dull ache settled behind my molars. There was no note, just the vibrant, gaudy display left carelessly on my pristine marble. A slow, deliberate movement brought my hand down, not to accept them, but to nudge the arrangement slightly askew, ensuring one of the petals drooped just so. I picked up a nearby, perfectly ordinary paperweight and began to turn it over and over between my fingers, the faint friction a small, satisfying release of tension. This whole gesture felt like an unnecessary imposition, a clumsy attempt to draw attention where none was warranted. I needed to make them understand the sheer waste of effort involved in such an amateur display.

[story 2]

When the delivery person handed over the flowers, I kept my expression meticulously neutral, a mask of polite indifference stretched taut over my features. The scent, overwhelmingly sweet, immediately grated on my nerves. I took the box with two fingers, my grip surprisingly firm, and didn't offer a word of thanks. As I walked back to my apartment, I consciously slowed my pace, letting my shoulders slump just a fraction more than necessary. Inside, I placed them on the entryway table, arranging them not in a pleasing cluster, but spaced out unevenly, forcing the eye to confront the sheer volume of unsolicited affection. I spent the next hour pacing the length of the living room, my footsteps echoing sharply on the hardwood floor, a restless energy bubbling just beneath the surface of my calm demeanor.

[story 3]

I found the flowers sitting on my desk, a jarring splash of color against the muted tones of my workspace. My breath hitched, a shallow, involuntary intake of air that tasted metallic. I didn't look at the arrangement itself; instead, my gaze swept over the empty space beside it, cataloging the lack of context. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat—a tight, controlled hiss. I reached out, my fingers brushing the velvety texture of a deep purple bloom, and then, with a sudden, sharp movement, I plucked one single stem and deliberately snapped it cleanly in half. I dropped the broken pieces onto the desk, the tiny sound of the break seeming disproportionately loud in the silence. I watched the way the petals scattered, a small act of calculated destruction that settled a strange, sharp satisfaction in my chest.
